title: Ringing Off
passage:
When the market-research firm Bellwether asked a thousand adults last winter how an unplanned telephone call now made them feel, the commonest reply was not warmth but a faint ___(1)___. To ring a friend unannounced, several respondents said, had come to seem a small ___(2)___ on their time — the very sort of intrusion that a written message would have spared them. Nils Ahlgren, the sociologist who designed the study, is wary of reading too much into a single winter's figures, yet the drift is hard to miss: across a decade the share of adults who said they would rather be telephoned than messaged has roughly halved.

___(3)___, some of this is plain convenience. A message can be answered at leisure, skimmed on a crowded train, set aside without offence; a call asks for the whole of you at once. But the mood his respondents describe is far from ___(4)___. They speak instead of guilt, of hesitation, of calls quietly put off for weeks — of a nagging sense that to telephone is to presume, to claim a stranger's attention without first asking leave. The old assumption that a voice was the warmest way to reach another person has, Ahlgren suggests, quietly ___(5)___.

Whether that change is a loss or a liberation he declines to say. What strikes him instead is how quickly the etiquette has hardened: a spoken invitation to talk, once the most ordinary thing in the world, now travels ahead of itself as a text, asking whether a call would be all right. He notes only that a generation raised to text first may discover, when some piece of news is finally too large for a screen, that the muscle for the unhurried call has wasted from long disuse — and that relearning it, late in the day, is harder than anyone tends to expect.
— Marguerite Lowe, society columnist

questions.rationale: Suffix-rhymed -ension noun set. The frame 'the commonest reply was not warmth but a faint ___', with 'not warmth but' flagging a negative-pole feeling, needs a word naming mild unease: 'a faint apprehension' is the locking collocation. comprehension = sense misfit (understanding is not a feeling one has toward an incoming call, and 'faint comprehension' does not answer 'how it made them feel'). suspension = collocation_misfit (a pause or a hanging-in-place, not an emotion; no idiomatic 'faint suspension' as a response). dimension = sense misfit (an aspect or measurement, not a feeling). Only 'apprehension' is idiomatic in the frame; the other three are real -ension nouns that fail on sense or collocation.

Collocation gap; all four are abstract -tion/-ction nouns (POS-uniform, shape-matched). The frame 'had come to seem a small ___ on their time', glossed by 'the very sort of intrusion that a written message would have spared them', calls for the fixed idiom 'an imposition on someone's time' (an unwelcome demand on it): 'imposition' locks. instruction = collocation_misfit (a direction or order; there is no idiom 'an instruction on their time'). injunction = collocation_misfit (a legal order or command; 'an injunction on their time' is not English). inspection = collocation_misfit (an examination; 'an inspection on their time' does not exist). Only 'imposition' collocates with 'on their time'.

Connective-logic gap; all four are sentence-initial adverbs (connective class matched). The gapped sentence grants the obvious, mundane explanation ('some of this is plain convenience') before the pivot 'But the mood his respondents describe is far from ___' introduces the deeper reason, so the link is concessive: 'Admittedly' is the only fit. Consequently = wrong_logic (signals cause-and-effect, but convenience is a reason behind the decline, not a consequence of the halved share stated just before). Meanwhile = wrong_logic (temporal 'at the same time', and no parallel time-frame is at issue). Conversely = wrong_logic (demands an opposite, but the sentence concedes rather than contradicts). Only 'Admittedly' sets up the concession the 'But' later answers.

Polarity gap with a suffix-rhymed -ant set. The frame 'the mood his respondents describe is far from ___' inverts the polarity: 'far from X' means the mood is the opposite of X, and the downstream evidence ('guilt', 'hesitation', 'calls quietly put off') shows a heavy, uneasy mood, so X must be the celebratory pole = 'jubilant'. hesitant = polarity_mirror + surface_word_match (it echoes 'hesitation' in the very next clause and a skimmer grabs it, but 'far from hesitant' would mean bold and decisive, contradicting the guilt and the postponed calls). reluctant = polarity_mirror (a negative word a skimmer keys onto, but 'far from reluctant' means eager, again contradicting the evidence). resonant = sense misfit (means echoing or evocative, the wrong dimension for a mood). Only 'jubilant' satisfies the inversion the frame demands.

Collocation gap with a suffix-rhymed -lapsed set. The frame 'The old assumption ... has, Ahlgren suggests, quietly ___' needs a verb meaning to fall gradually out of force or use: 'the assumption has quietly lapsed' is idiomatic. collapsed = sense/collocation misfit ('collapse' names a sudden, dramatic failure, which jars against 'quietly'; assumptions do not 'quietly collapse'). elapsed = sense misfit (only stretches of time elapse; 'the assumption has elapsed' is ungrammatical in sense). relapsed = sense misfit ('relapse' requires a subject that had recovered and slid back into a former bad state, which an assumption cannot do). Only 'lapsed' carries the gradual falling-into-disuse sense the frame supports.

generator_meta.topic: the decline of the unplanned personal telephone call and the social unease that now attaches to ringing someone unannounced
generator_meta.gap_marker_convention: ___(n)___ inline in passage; questions[] carries gap n as q_index n with prompt 'Gap (n)'
generator_meta.spelling_note: One variety held throughout: BrE ('offence' not 'offense'; no AmE-only spellings present).
generator_meta.schema_note: 5 gaps => 5 questions[]; matches the cloze invariant, M-SCHEMA's 1-5 range, and the bands.json cloze prompt/option classes. Keys spread B/C/A/D/B. See NOTES.md.
